brief.audience: curious adults who value evidence over sensational claims
brief.topic: Why some GPS devices showed date problems in 2019
brief.thesis: The GPS week counter rolled over as designed; vulnerable receiver software misread the repeated value.
brief.operatorNotes: Do not imply that GPS time reversed or that every receiver failed.

sources.title: GPS Week Number Rollover
sources.publisher: National Coordination Office for Space-Based Positioning, Navigation, and Timing
sources.evidenceNote: Official GPS overview of the legacy week counter, the 2019 rollover, affected equipment, and later rollover dates.

sources.title: Memorandum for U.S. Owners and Operators Using GPS to Obtain UTC Time
sources.evidenceNote: Government technical memorandum describing the ten-bit week field, its reset, and device implementation risks.

claims.text: The GPS week number rolled over in 2019, and some devices that did not handle the event correctly exhibited date problems.

sourceLinks.evidenceExcerpt: The official overview records the 2019 rollover and resulting problems in some GPS-enabled devices.
The memorandum warns that implementations which mishandle the rollover can report unreliable dates.

claims.text: The legacy civil GPS navigation message stores the week number in a ten-bit field that repeats after 1,024 weeks.

sourceLinks.evidenceExcerpt: The official overview explains the ten-bit field, its maximum value, and the repeating interval.
The memorandum independently describes the ten-bit parameter and its reset after 1,024 weeks.

claims.text: Equipment that interpreted the repeated week value incorrectly required software or firmware fixes.

sourceLinks.evidenceExcerpt: The official guidance states that affected equipment needed software or firmware patches.
The memorandum explains why receiver implementations, rather than the passage of time, create the ambiguity.

claims.text: Legacy receivers face another rollover in 2038, while newer civil messages use a larger week field.

sourceLinks.evidenceExcerpt: The official overview gives the next legacy rollover and explains the larger counter in newer messages.

claims.text: The event was a finite counter repeating its value, not time moving backward.

sourceLinks.evidenceExcerpt: The documented mechanism is a numerical week-field rollover in the navigation message.
The memorandum frames the issue as receiver interpretation of a repeated counter value.

script.title: The GPS clocks that looked haunted

beats.spokenText: On April 6, 2019, the legacy GPS week counter reset to zero, and some devices that handled it incorrectly showed the wrong date.
beats.onScreenText: Some GPS dates suddenly looked wrong

beats.spokenText: The legacy civil signal stores its week number in ten bits, leaving only a limited set of possible values.
beats.onScreenText: A finite counter inside the signal

beats.spokenText: That counter covers almost twenty years. When it rolled over, equipment that handled the ambiguity badly needed software patches.
Legacy receivers face another rollover in 2038, while newer navigation messages give the week counter more room.
beats.onScreenText: The next rollover is already known

beats.spokenText: The clocks looked haunted, but the mechanism was ordinary. The number reset, not time itself.
beats.onScreenText: The number reset. Time did not.

operations.text: A GPS DATE SUDDENLY LOOKED WRONG
THE NUMBER RESET. TIME DID NOT.
DEVICE INTERPRETATION CAUSED THE WRONG DATE
